one_sentence_contribution: Develops PrivaSeer, a million-document longitudinal dataset of privacy policies crawled over time, enabling temporal analysis of policy language evolution and compliance.
gap_it_leaves: Focuses on legal document text rather than general web content quality, leaving open questions about broader temporal shifts in web corpus characteristics.
key_claim: Privacy policies are becoming longer, harder to read, and more ambiguous over time, while severely underreporting the use of third parties and tracking technologies.
methodology: Built a crawler to discover, download, and extract archived privacy policies from the Internet Archive's Wayback Machine; curated a longitudinal dataset of 1,071,488 English-language privacy policies; developed an automated trend surfacing tool for temporal analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the evolving quality and transparency of web-scraped textual content like privacy policies contributes to information degradation in training corpora.

title: Online Media Archeology as AI Critique: Wikipedia's Links and Edits as Spatial and Temporal Fields
venue: AoIR Selected Papers of Internet Research
abstract: <jats:p>There have been many impulses to make sense of artificial intelligence, or what AI ‘is.’ We propose that media archeology on Wikipedia can aid in the sensemaking of AI. Wikipedia’s technicity - its linked article ecology together with its edit histories - makes it suitable to perform a media archeology, which already favors spaciousness and complexities. The starting point for the analysis is the ‘Artificial intelligence’ article on Wikipedia. We then operationalize the ‘See also’ section into a link ecology with categorizations and prominence over time, thus allowing us to map and explore the spatial field within which the artificial intelligence article has resided across its temporal dimension. This means approaching Wikipedia as a source of data about not only content but also the connections and ruptures. By ‘ruptures’ we mean changes by deletion or substitution in article associations. Ultimately, taking inspiration from media archeology’s presumption to unearth understandings of media technologies through their multiple dimensions and ruptures, we present our findings in terms of the ‘ideation company’ that AI keeps on Wikipedia. First, we notice the instability of the AI industry. Second, we observe a decline in the prominence of science fiction-related articles over time. The rise of articles under the umbrella of human enhancements can be seen in the most recent periods, as well as the rising prominence of articles related to threat recognition and accountability of AI. In our investigation, Wikipedia emerges as a (substantive) AI sense-making space as well as a media archeology instrument.</jats:p>
matched_query: (Wikipedia OR wiki) AND ("AI-generated content" OR synthetic content) AND (rise OR prevalence OR temporal OR longitudinal) AND year_published:[2023 TO 2026]
one_sentence_contribution: Applies media archeology to Wikipedia's link ecology and edit histories to construct a humanistic sensemaking framework for understanding AI as a cultural phenomenon.
gap_it_leaves: Qualitative and interpretive approach leaves empirical measurement of AI content growth or quality change unaddressed.

title: Evolution of diversity and dominance of companies in online activity.
abstract: Ever since the web began, the number of websites has been growing exponentially. These websites cover an ever-increasing range of online services that fill a variety of social and economic functions across a growing range of industries. Yet the networked nature of the web, combined with the economics of preferential attachment, increasing returns and global trade, suggest that over the long run a small number of competitive giants are likely to dominate each functional market segment, such as search, retail and social media. Here we perform a large scale longitudinal study to quantify the distribution of attention given in the online environment to competing organisations. In two large online social media datasets, containing more than 10 billion posts and spanning more than a decade, we tally the volume of external links posted towards the organisations' main domain name as a proxy for the online attention they receive. We also use the Common Crawl dataset-which contains the linkage patterns between more than a billion different websites-to study the patterns of link concentration over the past three years across the entire web. Lastly, we showcase the linking between economic, financial and market data by exploring the relationships between online attention on social media and the growth in enterprise value in the electric carmaker Tesla. Our analysis shows that despite the fact that we observe consistent growth in all the macro indicators-the total amount of online attention, in the number of organisations with an online presence, and in the functions they perform-we also observe that a smaller number of organisations account for an ever-increasing proportion of total user attention, usually with one large player dominating each function. These results highlight how evolution of the online economy involves innovation, diversity, and then competitive dominance.
matched_query: ("privacy policies over time" OR PrivaSeer OR "online activity") AND (web OR internet OR "privacy policy") AND (evolution OR longitudinal OR temporal) AND year_published:[2020 TO 2026]
one_sentence_contribution: Analyzes longitudinal data on web company diversity and market dominance, showing exponential website growth accompanied by increasing concentration among a few platform giants.
gap_it_leaves: Does not examine text quality or content composition of the web, leaving open how platform consolidation affects the linguistic diversity of training data.
key_claim: Online attention is increasingly concentrated among a small number of dominant companies, with the top 10 domains on Reddit growing from ~35% to ~60% of all attention between 2006 and 2019.
methodology: Large-scale longitudinal analysis of outbound weblinks from Reddit and Twitter (2006–2019) as proxies for online attention, combined with Common Crawl PageRank data and economic performance data of companies.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how increasing concentration of online attention toward dominant domains reduces the diversity of web-scraped training data available for language models.

title: Event Detection in Wikipedia Edit History Improved by Documents Web Based Automatic Assessment
abstract: A majority of current work in events extraction assumes the static nature of relationships in constant expertise knowledge bases. However, in collaborative environments, such as Wikipedia, information and systems are extraordinarily dynamic over time. In this work, we introduce a new approach for extracting complex structures of events from Wikipedia. We advocate a new model to represent events by engaging more than one entities that are generalizable to an arbitrary language. The evolution of an event is captured successfully primarily based on analyzing the user edits records in Wikipedia. Our work presents a basis for a singular class of evolution-aware entity-primarily based enrichment algorithms and will extensively increase the quality of entity accessibility and temporal retrieval for Wikipedia. We formalize this problem case and conduct comprehensive experiments on a real dataset of 1.8 million Wikipedia articles in order to show the effectiveness of our proposed answer. Furthermore, we suggest a new event validation automatic method relying on a supervised model to predict the presence of events in a non-annotated corpus. As the extra document source for event validation, we chose the Web due to its ease of accessibility and wide event coverage. Our outcomes display that we are capable of acquiring 70% precision evaluated on a manually annotated corpus. Ultimately, we conduct a comparison of our strategy versus the Current Event Portal of Wikipedia and discover that our proposed WikipEvent along with the usage of Co-References technique may be utilized to provide new and more data on events.
matched_query: (Wikipedia OR SERP OR "internet archive") AND ("quality change" OR readability OR evolution OR diversity) AND (temporal OR longitudinal OR "over time") AND year_published:[2020 TO 2026]
one_sentence_contribution: Proposes a new event-detection model for Wikipedia that leverages the dynamic temporal structure of edit histories and web-based document quality assessment.
gap_it_leaves: Focuses on event extraction accuracy rather than measuring longitudinal shifts in Wikipedia content quality or AI-generated text prevalence.
key_claim: Events can be effectively extracted from Wikipedia by modeling multi-entity event evolution through analysis of user edit histories.
methodology: Proposed an evolution-aware entity-based enrichment algorithm for event detection by analyzing Wikipedia user edit records; evaluated on a dataset of 1.8 million Wikipedia articles.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not consider whether the authenticity and temporal dynamics of human editorial behavior in Wikipedia provide qualitatively different training signals compared to static web-scraped content.

title: Consent in Crisis: The Rapid Decline of the AI Data Commons
abstract: General-purpose artificial intelligence (AI) systems are built on massive swathes of public web data, assembled into corpora such as C4, RefinedWeb, and Dolma. To our knowledge, we conduct the first, large-scale, longitudinal audit of the consent protocols for the web domains underlying AI training corpora. Our audit of 14,000 web domains provides an expansive view of crawlable web data and how codified data use preferences are changing over time. We observe a proliferation of AI-specific clauses to limit use, acute differences in restrictions on AI developers, as well as general inconsistencies between websites' expressed intentions in their Terms of Service and their robots.txt. We diagnose these as symptoms of ineffective web protocols, not designed to cope with the widespread re-purposing of the internet for AI. Our longitudinal analyses show that in a single year (2023-2024) there has been a rapid crescendo of data restrictions from web sources, rendering ~5%+ of all tokens in C4, or 28%+ of the most actively maintained, critical sources in C4, fully restricted from use. For Terms of Service crawling restrictions, a full 45% of C4 is now restricted. If respected or enforced, these restrictions are rapidly biasing the diversity, freshness, and scaling laws for general-purpose AI systems. We hope to illustrate the emerging crises in data consent, for both developers and creators. The foreclosure of much of the open web will impact not only commercial AI, but also non-commercial AI and academic research.
matched_query: all:"RefinedWeb" AND all:"web data"
one_sentence_contribution: First large-scale longitudinal audit of consent protocols across 14,000 web domains underlying AI training corpora, documenting the rapid proliferation of AI-specific data-use restrictions over time.
gap_it_leaves: Does not assess the downstream impact of consent restrictions on model quality or propose technical mechanisms for compliance-aware data curation.
key_claim: Approximately 5% of tokens in major AI training corpora have become restricted by robots.txt in under a year, and nearly 45% carry Terms of Service restrictions, signaling rapid decline of the open AI data commons.
methodology: Large-scale longitudinal audit of consent protocols (robots.txt and Terms of Service) across 14,000 web domains underlying C4, RefinedWeb, and Dolma corpora
limitation: The data ecosystem's inconsistencies between robots.txt and Terms of Service make it difficult to reliably determine actual consent signals; the audit captures codified preferences but not necessarily enforced ones.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the content being restricted disproportionately affects high-quality, diverse, or human-behavioral data versus low-quality or synthetic content, nor the downstream impact on model training quality.

title: Privacy Policies across the Ages: Content of Privacy Policies 1996–2021
venue: ACM Transactions on Privacy and Security
abstract: It is well known that most users do not read privacy policies but almost always tick the box to agree with them. While the length and readability of privacy policies have been well studied and many approaches for policy analysis based on natural language processing have been proposed, existing studies are limited in their depth and scope, often focusing on a small number of data practices at single point in time. In this article, we fill this gap by analyzing the 25-year history of privacy policies using machine learning and natural language processing and presenting a comprehensive analysis of policy contents. Specifically, we collect a large-scale longitudinal corpus of privacy policies from 1996 to 2021 and analyze their content in terms of the data practices they describe, the rights they grant to users, and the rights they reserve for their organizations. We pay particular attention to changes in response to recent privacy regulations such as the GDPR and CCPA. We observe some positive changes, such as reductions in data collection post-GDPR, but also a range of concerning data practices, such as widespread implicit data collection for which users have no meaningful choices or access rights. Our work is an important step toward making privacy policies machine readable on the user side, which would help users match their privacy preferences against the policies offered by web services.
matched_query: privacy policies over time web document corpus longitudinal
one_sentence_contribution: Presents a 25-year longitudinal analysis of privacy policy content using NLP, revealing how data practices and policy language have evolved from 1996 to 2021.
gap_it_leaves: Does not connect privacy policy evolution to AI training data governance or measure effects on web-corpus usability.
key_claim: Despite GDPR and CCPA introducing modest improvements, privacy policies show increasing use of location data, implicitly collected data, and data sharing with unnamed third parties over 25 years.
methodology: Longitudinal corpus analysis of over 50,000 English-language privacy policies (1996–2021) using 22 trained machine learning classifiers to categorize data practices and their attributes
limitation: The study is limited to English-language policies; additional limitations stem from the privacy policy retrieval process and content evaluation methods.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze how evolving privacy policies and data-sharing practices affect the quality and diversity of web-scraped AI training data or contribute to information degradation in training corpora.

title: "Way back then": A Data-driven View of 25+ years of Web Evolution
venue: Proceedings of the ACM Web Conference 2022
abstract: Since the inception of the first web page three decades back, the Web has evolved considerably, from static HTML pages in the beginning to the dynamic web pages of today, from mainly the text-based pages of the 1990s to today’s multimedia rich pages, etc.. Although much of this is known anecdotally, to our knowledge, there is no quantitative documentation of the extent and timing of these changes. This paper attempts to address this gap in the literature by looking at the top 100 Alexa websites for over 25 years from the Internet Archive or the “Wayback Machine”, archive.org. We study the changes in popularity, from Geocities and Yahoo! in the mid-to-late 1990s to the likes of Google, Facebook, and Tiktok of today. We also look at different categories of websites and their popularity over the years and find evidence for the decline in popularity of news and education-related websites, which have been replaced by streaming media and social networking sites. We explore the emergence and relative prevalence of different MIME-types (text vs. image vs. video vs. javascript and json) and study whether the use of text on the Internet is declining.
matched_query: (Wikipedia OR SERP OR "internet archive") AND ("quality change" OR readability OR evolution OR diversity) AND (temporal OR longitudinal OR "over time") AND year_published:[2020 TO 2026]
one_sentence_contribution: Provides a quantitative, data-driven characterization of how the top 100 websites have structurally and content-wise evolved over 25+ years using web archive data.
gap_it_leaves: Focuses on structural/technical web evolution rather than text quality or AI training data implications.
key_claim: Over 25+ years, the web has shifted from text-based news and education sites toward streaming media and social networking, with quantifiable declines in popularity of informational website categories.
methodology: Data-driven longitudinal analysis of the top 100 Alexa websites over 25+ years using archived snapshots from the Internet Archive (Wayback Machine)
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine how the compositional shift in web content (from informational to entertainment/social media) affects the diversity and quality of data available for AI training corpora.

title: Collective Response to Media Coverage of the COVID-19 Pandemic on Reddit and Wikipedia: Mixed-Methods Analysis
abstract: Background The exposure and consumption of information during epidemic outbreaks may alter people’s risk perception and trigger behavioral changes, which can ultimately affect the evolution of the disease. It is thus of utmost importance to map the dissemination of information by mainstream media outlets and the public response to this information. However, our understanding of this exposure-response dynamic during the COVID-19 pandemic is still limited. Objective The goal of this study is to characterize the media coverage and collective internet response to the COVID-19 pandemic in four countries: Italy, the United Kingdom, the United States, and Canada. Methods We collected a heterogeneous data set including 227,768 web-based news articles and 13,448 YouTube videos published by mainstream media outlets, 107,898 user posts and 3,829,309 comments on the social media platform Reddit, and 278,456,892 views of COVID-19–related Wikipedia pages. To analyze the relationship between media coverage, epidemic progression, and users’ collective web-based response, we considered a linear regression model that predicts the public response for each country given the amount of news exposure. We also applied topic modelling to the data set using nonnegative matrix factorization. Results Our results show that public attention, quantified as user activity on Reddit and active searches on Wikipedia pages, is mainly driven by media coverage; meanwhile, this activity declines rapidly while news exposure and COVID-19 incidence remain high. Furthermore, using an unsupervised, dynamic topic modeling approach, we show that while the levels of attention dedicated to different topics by media outlets and internet users are in good accordance, interesting deviations emerge in their temporal patterns. Conclusions Overall, our findings offer an additional key to interpret public perception and response to the current global health emergency and raise questions about the effects of attention saturation on people’s collective awareness and risk perception and thus on their tendencies toward behavioral change.
matched_query: Wikipedia quality change web over time measurement
one_sentence_contribution: Uses a mixed-methods approach to map mainstream media COVID-19 coverage and measure the collective public response on Reddit and Wikipedia over time.
gap_it_leaves: Temporal analysis is limited to a single health crisis and does not generalize to broader web-text quality trends relevant to AI training corpora.
key_claim: Mainstream media coverage of COVID-19 and collective internet responses on Reddit and Wikipedia are temporally coupled, revealing an exposure-response dynamic across four countries.
methodology: Mixed-methods analysis of 227,768 news articles, 13,448 YouTube videos, 107,898 Reddit posts, 3,829,309 Reddit comments, and 278,456,892 Wikipedia page views across Italy, UK, US, and Canada
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the collective behavioral signals captured on Reddit and Wikipedia represent authentic human social data that could serve as higher-quality training signals compared to standard web-scraped corpora.

title: Social IQa: Commonsense Reasoning about Social Interactions
abstract: Social IQa is a benchmark for commonsense reasoning about social interactions, focusing on questions that require understanding people's actions, intents, and reactions in everyday situations.
manual_core_reason: Social IQa is the flagged missing benchmark anchor for socially grounded reasoning in Beat 4.
one_sentence_contribution: Introduces Social IQa, a large-scale benchmark for evaluating commonsense reasoning about social interactions, covering people's actions, intents, and reactions.
gap_it_leaves: Focuses on text-based, English social reasoning without cross-cultural or multimodal evaluation.
key_claim: Large pretrained language models achieve only up to 65% accuracy on Social IQA compared to ~90% human performance, showing social commonsense reasoning remains challenging for AI.
methodology: Crowdsourced 38k multiple-choice QA pairs about social situations with adversarial question-switching to reduce stylistic artifacts; finetuned OpenAI-GPT and BERT models as baselines.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the social commonsense captured in web-scraped training corpora degrades over time or whether authentic human behavioral data would improve model performance on such tasks.

title: Neural Theory-of-Mind? On the Limits of Social Intelligence in Large LMs
abstract: Social intelligence and Theory of Mind (TOM), i.e., the ability to reason about the different mental states, intents, and reactions of all people involved, allows humans to effectively navigate and understand everyday social interactions. As NLP systems are used in increasingly complex social situations, their ability to grasp social dynamics becomes crucial.In this work, we examine the open question of social intelligence and Theory of Mind in modern NLP systems from an empirical and theorybased perspective. We show that one of today's largest language models (GPT-3; Brown et al., 2020) lacks this kind of social intelligence out-of-the box, using two tasks: SocialIQa (Sap et al., 2019), which measure models' ability to understand intents and reactions of participants of social interactions, and ToMi (Le, Boureau, and Nickel, 2019), which measures whether models can infer mental states and realities of participants of situations.Our results show that models struggle substantially at these Theory of Mind tasks, with well-below-human accuracies of 55% and 60% on SocialIQa and ToMi, respectively. To conclude, we draw on theories from pragmatics to contextualize this shortcoming of large language models, by examining the limitations stemming from their data, neural architecture, and training paradigms. Challenging the prevalent narrative that only scale is needed, we posit that person-centric NLP approaches might be more effective towards neural Theory of Mind.
matched_query: SocialIQA social reasoning benchmark evaluation
one_sentence_contribution: Empirically and theoretically examines the limits of social intelligence and Theory of Mind in large language models, finding significant gaps relative to human performance.
gap_it_leaves: Does not propose training interventions or data curation strategies to improve social reasoning in LLMs.
key_claim: Out-of-the-box GPT-3 models lag behind humans by over 30% on social intelligence tasks and struggle with mental state reasoning, suggesting that scaling model size alone is insufficient for Theory of Mind.
methodology: Zero-shot and few-shot evaluation of GPT-3 variants (Ada, Curie, Davinci) on SocialIQA and ToMI benchmarks, with pragmatics-based analysis of pretraining limitations.
limitation: Authors acknowledge they could only access GPT-3 via API and lacked computational resources to run open-source alternatives like OPT or access PaLM, though they hypothesize results would be similar based on BIG-Bench findings.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the static text pretraining paradigm's limitations stem specifically from information degradation in web-scraped data versus the fundamental absence of grounded social behavioral signals.
theoretical_framework: Pragmatics-based analysis of language model pretraining limitations

title: CoMMET: To What Extent Can LLMs Perform Theory of Mind Tasks?
abstract: Theory of Mind (ToM)-the ability to reason about the mental states of oneself and others-is a cornerstone of human social intelligence. As Large Language Models (LLMs) become ubiquitous in real-world applications, validating their capacity for this level of social reasoning is essential for effective and natural interactions. However, existing benchmarks for assessing ToM in LLMs are limited; most rely solely on text inputs and focus narrowly on belief-related tasks. In this paper, we propose a new multimodal benchmark dataset, CoMMET, a Comprehensive Mental states and Moral Evaluation Task inspired by the Theory of Mind Booklet Task. CoMMET expands the scope of evaluation by covering a broader range of mental states and introducing multi-turn testing. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first multimodal dataset to evaluate ToM in a multi-turn conversational setting. Through a comprehensive assessment of LLMs across different families and sizes, we analyze the strengths and limitations of current models and identify directions for future improvement. Our work offers a deeper understanding of the social cognitive capabilities of modern LLMs.
matched_query: all:"social reasoning" AND all:"benchmark" AND all:"language model"
one_sentence_contribution: Proposes CoMMET, a multimodal Theory of Mind benchmark for LLMs that goes beyond text and belief tasks to assess broader social reasoning capabilities.
gap_it_leaves: Does not investigate training data composition or fine-tuning strategies that might improve ToM performance.
key_claim: Existing ToM benchmarks for LLMs are limited to text-only belief tasks; CoMMET provides a multimodal, multi-turn evaluation covering all ATOMS mental states and moral reasoning, revealing significant LLM deficits.
methodology: Created a multimodal benchmark (CoMMET) inspired by psychological Theory of Mind Booklet Tasks, with multi-turn conversational evaluation; tested LLaMA-3.2-11B, LLaMA-4-Maverick, and other LLMs with and without image inputs.
limitation: Authors note issues with image generation quality including incorrect spatial relationships and occasional feedback misplacement in generated turns.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether LLM failures on social reasoning tasks trace back to the quality or diversity degradation of social behavioral data in web-scraped training corpora.

title: Social-R1: Towards Human-like Social Reasoning in LLMs
abstract: While large language models demonstrate remarkable capabilities across numerous domains, social intelligence - the capacity to perceive social cues, infer mental states, and generate appropriate responses - remains a critical challenge, particularly for enabling effective human-AI collaboration and developing AI that truly serves human needs. Current models often rely on superficial patterns rather than genuine social reasoning. We argue that cultivating human-like social intelligence requires training with challenging cases that resist shortcut solutions. To this end, we introduce ToMBench-Hard, an adversarial benchmark designed to provide hard training examples for social reasoning. Building on this, we propose Social-R1, a reinforcement learning framework that aligns model reasoning with human cognition through multi-dimensional rewards. Unlike outcome-based RL, Social-R1 supervises the entire reasoning process, enforcing structural alignment, logical integrity, and information density. Results show that our approach enables a 4B parameter model to surpass much larger counterparts and generalize robustly across eight diverse benchmarks. These findings demonstrate that challenging training cases with trajectory-level alignment offer a path toward efficient and reliable social intelligence.
matched_query: all:"social reasoning" AND all:"benchmark" AND all:"language model"
one_sentence_contribution: Introduces Social-R1, a framework applying reinforcement learning to cultivate human-like social intelligence in LLMs by going beyond superficial pattern matching.
gap_it_leaves: Does not systematically study the role of training data quality or human annotation in achieving genuine social reasoning.
key_claim: Current LLMs rely on superficial patterns rather than genuine social reasoning, and training with adversarial hard examples via reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback significantly improves social reasoning robustness.
methodology: Introduced ToMBench-Hard adversarial benchmark with perturbation-based hard examples; trained Social-R1 model using reinforcement learning from verifiable feedback (RLVF) on social reasoning tasks; conducted stage-wise diagnosis using Social Information Processing (SIP) framework and perturbation robustness analysis.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether incorporating physically-verified authentic human social behavioral data, rather than adversarially augmented text-based examples, could address the interpretation bottleneck in social reasoning.

title: S$^3$IT: A Benchmark for Spatially Situated Social Intelligence Test
abstract: The integration of embodied agents into human environments demands embodied social intelligence: reasoning over both social norms and physical constraints. However, existing evaluations fail to address this integration, as they are limited to either disembodied social reasoning (e.g., in text) or socially-agnostic physical tasks. Both approaches fail to assess an agent's ability to integrate and trade off both physical and social constraints within a realistic, embodied context. To address this challenge, we introduce Spatially Situated Social Intelligence Test (S$^{3}$IT), a benchmark specifically designed to evaluate embodied social intelligence. It is centered on a novel and challenging seat-ordering task, requiring an agent to arrange seating in a 3D environment for a group of large language model-driven (LLM-driven) NPCs with diverse identities, preferences, and intricate interpersonal relationships. Our procedurally extensible framework generates a vast and diverse scenario space with controllable difficulty, compelling the agent to acquire preferences through active dialogue, perceive the environment via autonomous exploration, and perform multi-objective optimization within a complex constraint network. We evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs on S$^{3}$IT and found that they still struggle with this problem, showing an obvious gap compared with the human baseline. Results imply that LLMs have deficiencies in spatial intelligence, yet simultaneously demonstrate their ability to achieve near human-level competence in resolving conflicts that possess explicit textual cues.
matched_query: all:"social reasoning" AND all:"benchmark" AND all:"language model"
one_sentence_contribution: Presents S³IT, a benchmark that jointly evaluates social and spatial reasoning for embodied agents, addressing the gap between disembodied social reasoning and socially-agnostic physical tasks.
gap_it_leaves: Embodied setting limits applicability to purely text-based LLM social reasoning evaluation.
key_claim: State-of-the-art LLMs follow explicit social rules but exhibit a critical deficit in integrating spatial and social reasoning in embodied contexts.
methodology: Procedural generation of 3D simulated environments with NPCs and a multi-phase evaluation pipeline testing embodied agents on spatial and social constraint integration.
limitation: Interactive testing means varying interaction processes influence performance; human evaluation relied on only three experts, limiting generalizability; empirical curve used to penalize models may be imprecise.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether training on web-scraped data versus physically-grounded authentic human behavioral data contributes to the spatial-social reasoning deficit.

title: Are Vision Language Models Cross-Cultural Theory of Mind Reasoners?
abstract: Theory of Mind (ToM) - the ability to attribute beliefs and intents to others - is fundamental for social intelligence, yet Vision-Language Model (VLM) evaluations remain largely Western-centric. In this work, we introduce CulturalToM-VQA, a benchmark of 5,095 visually situated ToM probes across diverse cultural contexts, rituals, and social norms. Constructed through a frontier proprietary MLLM, human-verified pipeline, the dataset spans a taxonomy of six ToM tasks and four complexity levels. We benchmark 10 VLMs (2023-2025) and observe a significant performance leap: while earlier models struggle, frontier models achieve high accuracy (>93%). However, significant limitations persist: models struggle with false belief reasoning (19-83% accuracy) and show high regional variance (20-30% gaps). Crucially, we find that SOTA models exhibit social desirability bias - systematically favoring semantically positive answer choices over negative ones. Ablation experiments reveal that some frontier models rely heavily on parametric social priors, frequently defaulting to safety-aligned predictions. Furthermore, while Chain-of-Thought prompting aids older models, it yields minimal gains for newer ones. Overall, our work provides a testbed for cross-cultural social reasoning, underscoring that despite architectural gains, achieving robust, visually grounded understanding remains an open challenge.
matched_query: all:"social reasoning" AND all:"benchmark" AND all:"language model"
one_sentence_contribution: Introduces CulturalToM-VQA, a visually situated, cross-cultural Theory of Mind benchmark with 5,095 probes, revealing that VLMs underperform on non-Western cultural contexts.
gap_it_leaves: Does not address how culturally diverse training data or curation strategies could improve cross-cultural ToM performance.
key_claim: Frontier VLMs achieve over 93% accuracy on explicit ToM tasks across cultures but exhibit visual insensitivity, maintaining high performance even without images due to pretrained world knowledge and social desirability bias.
methodology: Construction of CulturalToM-VQA benchmark (5,095 probes) via frontier MLLM generation with human verification; evaluation of 10 VLMs using zero-shot, zero-shot CoT, and compositional CoT prompting with visual ablation.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the models' reliance on pretrained textual priors over visual grounding reflects information degradation or homogeneity in web-scraped training corpora.
theoretical_framework: Theory of Mind taxonomy with six ToM task types and four complexity levels

title: MetaMind: Modeling Human Social Thoughts with Metacognitive Multi-Agent Systems
abstract: Human social interactions depend on the ability to infer others' unspoken intentions, emotions, and beliefs-a cognitive skill grounded in the psychological concept of Theory of Mind (ToM). While large language models (LLMs) excel in semantic understanding tasks, they struggle with the ambiguity and contextual nuance inherent in human communication. To bridge this gap, we introduce MetaMind, a multi-agent framework inspired by psychological theories of metacognition, designed to emulate human-like social reasoning. MetaMind decomposes social understanding into three collaborative stages: (1) a Theory-of-Mind Agent generates hypotheses about user mental states (e.g., intent, emotion), (2) a Moral Agent refines these hypotheses using cultural norms and ethical constraints, and (3) a Response Agent generates contextually appropriate responses while validating alignment with inferred intent. Our framework achieves state-of-the-art performance across three challenging benchmarks, with 35.7% improvement in real-world social scenarios and 6.2% gain in ToM reasoning. Notably, it enables LLMs to match human-level performance on key ToM tasks for the first time. Ablation studies confirm the necessity of all components, which showcase the framework's ability to balance contextual plausibility, social appropriateness, and user adaptation. This work advances AI systems toward human-like social intelligence, with applications in empathetic dialogue and culturally sensitive interactions. Code is available at https://github.com/XMZhangAI/MetaMind.
matched_query: all:"social reasoning" AND all:"benchmark" AND all:"language model"
one_sentence_contribution: MetaMind is a metacognitive multi-agent framework that models human social thoughts by inferring others' intentions, emotions, and beliefs using Theory of Mind principles.
gap_it_leaves: Does not address how such systems scale or generalize across diverse cultural contexts for social reasoning.
key_claim: MetaMind, a metacognitive multi-agent framework, enables LLMs to match human performance on key Theory of Mind tasks by decomposing social reasoning into hypothesis generation, norm-aware refinement, and validated response generation.
methodology: Multi-agent framework with three collaborative stages (Theory-of-Mind Agent, Moral Agent, Validation Agent) inspired by metacognitive psychology, evaluated on ToM and social cognition benchmarks.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the quality or diversity of underlying training data (web-scraped vs. authentic human behavioral data) limits the social reasoning capabilities that MetaMind attempts to scaffold.
theoretical_framework: Metacognitive theory of social reasoning decomposed into Theory of Mind, moral reasoning, and self-monitoring stages

title: MimeQA: Towards Socially-Intelligent Nonverbal Foundation Models
abstract: As AI becomes more closely integrated with peoples' daily activities, socially intelligent AI that can understand and interact seamlessly with humans in daily lives is increasingly important. However, current works in AI social reasoning all rely on language-only or language-dominant approaches to benchmark and training models, resulting in systems that are improving in verbal communication but struggle with nonverbal social understanding. To address this limitation, we tap into a novel data source rich in nonverbal social interactions -- mime videos. Mimes refer to the art of expression through gesture and movement without spoken words, which presents unique challenges and opportunities in interpreting nonverbal social communication. We contribute a new dataset called MimeQA, obtained by sourcing ~8 hours of videos clips from YouTube and developing a comprehensive video question-answering benchmark comprising 806 carefully annotated and verified question-answer pairs, designed to probe nonverbal social reasoning capabilities. Using MimeQA, we evaluate state-of-the-art video large language models (VideoLLMs) and find that they achieve low accuracy, generally ranging from 20-30%, while humans score 86%. Our analysis reveals that VideoLLMs often fail to ground imagined objects and over-rely on the text prompt while ignoring subtle nonverbal interactions. We hope to inspire future work in AI models that embody true social intelligence capable of interpreting non-verbal human interactions.
matched_query: all:"social reasoning" AND all:"benchmark" AND all:"language model"
one_sentence_contribution: MimeQA introduces a benchmark and dataset for evaluating nonverbal social reasoning in multimodal foundation models, addressing the gap left by language-dominant approaches.
gap_it_leaves: Focuses on nonverbal cues but leaves open how to integrate verbal and nonverbal signals jointly for richer social intelligence evaluation.
key_claim: Current video LLMs struggle with nonverbal social understanding in mime videos, and fine-tuning improves higher-level social reasoning but fails to ground imagined objects.
methodology: Construction of MIMEQA benchmark from mime videos; evaluation of closed-source (Gemini-1.5-Pro, Gemini-2.5-Pro, GPT-4o) and open-source (Qwen2.5-VL, LLaVA-Video, InternVL2.5, VideoLLaMA3) VideoLLMs with standardized prompts and fine-tuning experiments.
limitation: Dataset is limited in size; many expressive nonverbal modalities (dance, cultural performances, music) remain unexplored; developing robust social reasoning from small data regimes is an open challenge.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the linguistic bias of current models stems from the dominance of text-based web-scraped data in pretraining and the absence of authentic embodied human behavioral signals.

title: Multi-speaker Attention Alignment for Multimodal Social Interaction
abstract: Understanding social interaction in video requires reasoning over a dynamic interplay of verbal and non-verbal cues: who is speaking, to whom, and with what gaze or gestures. While Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) are natural candidates, simply adding visual inputs yields surprisingly inconsistent gains on social tasks. Our quantitative analysis of cross-modal attention inside state-of-the-art MLLMs reveals a core failure mode: in multi-speaker scenes, visual and textual tokens lack speaker-consistent alignment, exhibiting substantially weaker cross-modal attention than in object-centric images. To address this, we propose a multimodal multi-speaker attention alignment method that can be integrated into existing MLLMs. First, we introduce dynamic cross-modal head selection to identify attention heads most responsible for grounding. Then, an adaptive social-aware attention bias, computed from existing attention patterns and speaker locations, is injected into the attention mechanism. This bias reinforces alignment between a speaker's visual representation and their utterances without introducing trainable parameters or architectural changes. We integrate our method into three distinct MLLMs (LLaVA-NeXT-Video, Qwen2.5-VL, and InternVL3) and evaluate on three benchmarks (TVQA+, MMSI, OnlineMMSI). Across four social tasks, results demonstrate that our approach improves the ability of MLLMs and achieves state-of-the-art results. Attention visualizations confirm our method successfully focuses the model on speaker-relevant regions, enabling more robust multi-party social reasoning. Our implementation and model will be available at https://github.com/ut-vision/SocialInteraction.
matched_query: all:"social reasoning" AND all:"benchmark" AND all:"language model"
one_sentence_contribution: Proposes a multi-speaker attention alignment method for multimodal LLMs to improve social interaction understanding in video by correcting cross-modal attention failures in multi-speaker scenes.
gap_it_leaves: Leaves open how to handle very large group interactions and more complex social dynamics beyond speaker-level attention alignment.
key_claim: MLLMs exhibit weak cross-modal attention alignment in multi-speaker scenes, and a proposed multi-speaker attention alignment method improves social interaction understanding by up to 3.2%.
methodology: Dynamic cross-modal head selection and adaptive social-aware attention bias applied to MLLMs, evaluated on multi-speaker video understanding tasks
limitation: Relies on bounding box annotations for speaker alignment; future work aims to reduce annotation costs by leveraging inherent grounding abilities of MLLMs.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether training data for MLLMs suffers from underrepresentation of authentic multi-party social interactions compared to scripted or web-scraped content.

title: Clever Hans or Neural Theory of Mind? Stress Testing Social Reasoning in Large Language Models
abstract: Natalie Shapira, Mosh Levy, Seyed Hossein Alavi, Xuhui Zhou, Yejin Choi, Yoav Goldberg, Maarten Sap, Vered Shwartz. Proceedings of the 18th Conference of the European Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers). 2024.
matched_query: SocialIQA social reasoning benchmark evaluation
one_sentence_contribution: Stress-tests LLMs on social reasoning and Theory of Mind tasks, revealing that apparent competence may reflect dataset artifacts rather than genuine social reasoning ability.
gap_it_leaves: Does not propose concrete remedies for the identified spurious correlations or how to build more robust social reasoning benchmarks.
key_claim: LLMs exhibit limited and non-robust Theory of Mind abilities, relying on shallow heuristics rather than generalized social reasoning, as shown by failures on adversarial examples.
methodology: Zero-shot evaluation of 15 LLMs across 6 ToM tasks with adversarial probing and sensitivity analysis of probing methods
limitation: Benchmark scope is limited to 6 test sets; some test questions may be ambiguous; only zero-shot evaluation was used.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether LLMs' shallow social reasoning stems from the lack of authentic human social behavioral signals in web-scraped training data.
theoretical_framework: Neural Theory of Mind (N-ToM) evaluation framework

title: On Curriculum Learning for Commonsense Reasoning
venue: Proceedings of the 2022 Conference of the North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Human Language Technologies
abstract: Commonsense reasoning tasks follow a standard paradigm of finetuning pretrained language models on the target task data, where samples are introduced to the model in a random order during training. However, recent research suggests that data order can have a significant impact on the performance of finetuned models for natural language understanding. Hence, we examine the effect of a humanlike easy-to-difficult curriculum during finetuning of language models for commonsense reasoning tasks. We use paced curriculum learning to rank data and sample training mini-batches with increasing levels of difficulty from the ranked dataset during finetuning. Further, we investigate the effect of an adaptive curriculum, i.e., the data ranking is dynamically updated during training based on the current state of the learner model. We use a teacher model to measure difficulty of each sample and experiment with three measures based on question answering probability, variability and out-ofdistribution. To understand the effectiveness of curriculum learning in various scenarios, we apply it on full model fine-tuning as well as parameter-efficient prompt-tuning settings. Our results show that fixed as well as adaptive curriculum learning significantly improve performance for five commonsense reasoning tasks, i.e., SocialIQA, CosmosQA, CODAH, HellaSwag, WinoGrande in both tuning settings. Further, we find that prioritizing the difficult samples in the tail end of training improves generalization to unseen in-domain data as well as out-of-domain data. Our work provides evidence and encourages research into curriculum learning for commonsense reasoning. 1 .
matched_query: SocialIQA social reasoning benchmark evaluation
one_sentence_contribution: Examines the effect of easy-to-difficult curriculum learning during fine-tuning of language models on commonsense reasoning benchmarks including SocialIQA, showing modest but task-dependent gains.
gap_it_leaves: Does not explore curriculum learning for larger-scale or instruction-tuned models, nor for more nuanced social reasoning tasks.
key_claim: Adaptive curriculum learning during fine-tuning of pretrained language models improves commonsense reasoning accuracy by up to 3.17% over random data ordering.
methodology: Paced curriculum learning with fixed and adaptive difficulty scoring (QAP metric) applied to RoBERTa-LARGE fine-tuning on multiple-choice QA tasks
limitation: Results are limited to multiple-choice commonsense QA; adaptive CL is expensive for larger datasets due to repeated re-ranking.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the quality or diversity of the underlying pretraining corpus affects the effectiveness of curriculum learning for commonsense reasoning.

title: TSGP: Two-Stage Generative Prompting for Unsupervised Commonsense Question Answering
abstract: Without training on labeled task data, unsupervised commonsense question answering seems challenging since it requires commonsense knowledge beyond the context of questions. Previous methods typically retrieved from traditional knowledge bases or used pre-trained language models (PrLMs) to generate fixed types of knowledge, which have poor generalization ability.In this paper, we aim to address the above limitation by leveraging the implicit knowledge stored in PrLMs and propose a two-stage prompt-based unsupervised commonsense question answering framework (TSGP). We first use knowledge generation prompts to generate the knowledge required for questions with unlimited types and possible candidate answers independent of specified choices. Then, we further utilize answer generation prompts to generate possible candidate answers independent of specified choices. Experimental results and analysis on three different commonsense reasoning tasks, CommonsenseQA, OpenBookQA, and SocialIQA, demonstrate that TSGP significantly improves the reasoning ability of language models in unsupervised settings.
matched_query: SocialIQA social reasoning benchmark evaluation
one_sentence_contribution: TSGP uses two-stage generative prompting with pre-trained language models to perform unsupervised commonsense question answering without labeled data, evaluated on benchmarks including SocialIQA.
gap_it_leaves: Does not address how generated knowledge quality degrades when social or cultural context is underrepresented in the pre-trained model.
key_claim: A two-stage generative prompting framework (TSGP) achieves state-of-the-art unsupervised commonsense QA by generating unlimited types of knowledge and candidate answers from pretrained language models.
methodology: Two-stage prompting with GPT for knowledge generation followed by answer generation and semantic similarity scoring against candidate choices, without labeled task data
limitation: Prompt design is not optimized (examples randomly sampled); limited computing resources prevented evaluation on larger language models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the implicit commonsense knowledge in pretrained LMs degrades due to information homogeneity or tail collapse in web-scraped training corpora.

title: Self-Instruct: Aligning Language Models with Self-Generated Instructions
abstract: Yizhong Wang, Yeganeh Kordi, Swaroop Mishra, Alisa Liu, Noah A. Smith, Daniel Khashabi, Hannaneh Hajishirzi. Proceedings of the 61st Annual Meeting of the Association for Computational Linguistics (Volume 1: Long Papers). 2023.
matched_query: social commonsense reasoning language model evaluation
manual_core_reason: Self-Instruct is a foundational synthetic-instruction-data composition anchor for alignment fine-tuning.
one_sentence_contribution: Self-Instruct proposes a method to bootstrap instruction-following data by having LLMs generate and filter their own instructions, substantially reducing reliance on human-labeled instruction data.
gap_it_leaves: Does not analyze the long-term quality degradation when iteratively training on self-generated instructions, which connects to model collapse concerns.
manual_override_reason: Self-Instruct is a direct instruction-data composition anchor for alignment fine-tuning.
key_claim: Bootstrapping instruction data from a language model's own generations improves GPT-3's instruction-following by 33% on SUPER-NI without relying on human-written instructions.
methodology: Generating instructions, inputs, and outputs from vanilla GPT-3, filtering invalid/similar samples, and fine-tuning the original model on 52K self-generated instructions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether self-generated synthetic instruction data suffers from reduced diversity or entropy collapse compared to authentic human-authored data.

title: OpenAssistant Conversations -- Democratizing Large Language Model Alignment
abstract: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with human preferences has proven to drastically improve usability and has driven rapid adoption as demonstrated by ChatGPT. Alignment techniques such as supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) greatly reduce the required skill and domain knowledge to effectively harness the capabilities of LLMs, increasing their accessibility and utility across various domains. However, state-of-the-art alignment techniques like RLHF rely on high-quality human feedback data, which is expensive to create and often remains proprietary. In an effort to democratize research on large-scale alignment, we release OpenAssistant Conversations, a human-generated, human-annotated assistant-style conversation corpus consisting of 161,443 messages in 35 different languages, annotated with 461,292 quality ratings, resulting in over 10,000 complete and fully annotated conversation trees. The corpus is a product of a worldwide crowd-sourcing effort involving over 13,500 volunteers. Models trained on OpenAssistant Conversations show consistent improvements on standard benchmarks over respective base models. We release our code and data under a fully permissive licence.
matched_query: data quality LLM training curation pretraining scaling
tldr: OpenAssistant Conversations is released, a human-generated, human-annotated assistant-style conversation corpus consisting of 161,443 messages in 35 different languages, annotated with 461,292 quality ratings, resulting in over 10,000 complete and fully annotated conversation trees.
manual_core_reason: OpenAssistant Conversations is a human / open alignment-data anchor for fine-tuning composition.
one_sentence_contribution: OpenAssistant presents a large-scale human-annotated conversation dataset and demonstrates that open, democratized RLHF pipelines can align LLMs comparably to proprietary approaches.
gap_it_leaves: Does not investigate the scalability ceiling of human annotation or how quality degrades as community-contributed data grows.
manual_override_reason: OpenAssistant Conversations is a foundational open alignment-data corpus and belongs in J for this thesis.
key_claim: A large-scale, crowd-sourced open dataset of human feedback conversations can enable competitive LLM alignment, democratizing access to RLHF-quality data.
methodology: Crowd-sourced collection of multi-turn human assistant conversations with quality guidelines, followed by supervised fine-tuning and RLHF on the resulting OpenAssistant Conversations dataset.
limitation: Reward models were trained on rankings of human-generated messages rather than model-generated messages (as in InstructGPT), which may explain why RLHF did not uniformly improve over SFT models.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not analyze how crowd-sourced web-collected feedback data may introduce biases or diversity loss relative to physically-verified authentic behavioral signals.

title: LIMA: Less Is More for Alignment
abstract: Large language models are trained in two stages: (1) unsupervised pretraining from raw text, to learn general-purpose representations, and (2) large scale instruction tuning and reinforcement learning, to better align to end tasks and user preferences. We measure the relative importance of these two stages by training LIMA, a 65B parameter LLaMa language model fine-tuned with the standard supervised loss on only 1,000 carefully curated prompts and responses, without any reinforcement learning or human preference modeling. LIMA demonstrates remarkably strong performance, learning to follow specific response formats from only a handful of examples in the training data, including complex queries that range from planning trip itineraries to speculating about alternate history. Moreover, the model tends to generalize well to unseen tasks that did not appear in the training data. In a controlled human study, responses from LIMA are either equivalent or strictly preferred to GPT-4 in 43% of cases; this statistic is as high as 58% when compared to Bard and 65% versus DaVinci003, which was trained with human feedback. Taken together, these results strongly suggest that almost all knowledge in large language models is learned during pretraining, and only limited instruction tuning data is necessary to teach models to produce high quality output.
matched_query: LIMA quality over quantity instruction tuning alignment
manual_core_reason: LIMA is a quality-over-quantity fine-tuning anchor for Beat 4 data composition.
one_sentence_contribution: LIMA demonstrates that fine-tuning on only 1,000 carefully curated examples suffices for strong alignment, suggesting data quality vastly outweighs quantity in instruction tuning.

contradicts.reason: Challenges the large-scale human annotation paradigm by showing minimal curated data achieves comparable alignment

gap_it_leaves: Does not address how to systematically identify or generate high-quality curation criteria at scale, nor the effect on downstream social reasoning tasks.
key_claim: Fine-tuning a 65B LLaMA model on only 1,000 carefully curated examples without RLHF produces competitive performance with state-of-the-art models, supporting the Superficial Alignment Hypothesis.
methodology: Supervised fine-tuning of LLaMA-65B on 1,000 manually curated prompt-response pairs, evaluated via human preference comparison against DaVinci003, Alpaca-65B, and GPT-4.
limitation: The mental effort to construct high-quality curated examples is significant and difficult to scale; LIMA is not as robust as product-grade models and can produce weak responses under adversarial prompts or unlucky decoding.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether the quality of pretraining data (e.g., web-scraped vs. authentic human behavioral data) affects the sufficiency of minimal alignment fine-tuning.

title: WizardLM: Empowering large pre-trained language models to follow complex instructions
abstract: Training large language models (LLMs) with open-domain instruction following data brings colossal success. However, manually creating such instruction data is very time-consuming and labor-intensive. Moreover, humans may struggle to produce high-complexity instructions. In this paper, we show an avenue for creating large amounts of instruction data with varying levels of complexity using LLM instead of humans. Starting with an initial set of instructions, we use our proposed Evol-Instruct to rewrite them step by step into more complex instructions. Then, we mix all generated instruction data to fine-tune LLaMA. We call the resulting model WizardLM. Human evaluations on a complexity-balanced test bed and Vicuna's testset show that instructions from Evol-Instruct are superior to human-created ones. By analyzing the human evaluation results of the high complexity part, we demonstrate that outputs from our WizardLM are preferred to outputs from OpenAI ChatGPT. In GPT-4 automatic evaluation, WizardLM achieves more than 90\% capacity of ChatGPT on 17 out of 29 skills. Even though WizardLM still lags behind ChatGPT in some aspects, our findings suggest that fine-tuning with AI-evolved instructions is a promising direction for enhancing LLMs. Our code and data are public at https://github.com/nlpxucan/WizardLM
matched_query: social commonsense reasoning language model evaluation
tldr: The findings suggest that fine-tuning with AI-evolved instructions is a promising direction for enhancing LLMs, and it is demonstrated that outputs from the authors' WizardLM are preferred to outputs from OpenAI ChatGPT.
manual_core_reason: WizardLM is a high-signal instruction-data construction / complexity-mixture anchor.
one_sentence_contribution: WizardLM introduces Evol-Instruct, an LLM-driven method to automatically generate instruction data of increasing complexity, enabling fine-tuned models to follow complex instructions more effectively.

builds_on.reason: Extends the self-generated instruction paradigm of Self-Instruct by adding complexity evolution

gap_it_leaves: Does not evaluate whether increasingly complex synthetic instructions introduce distributional artifacts or hasten model collapse in iterative training.
manual_override_reason: WizardLM is a direct instruction-data generation / complexity-mixture anchor.
key_claim: Evol-Instruct, which uses LLMs to iteratively rewrite instructions into more complex versions, produces instruction data that enables WizardLM to outperform Alpaca and Vicuna on multiple benchmarks.
methodology: Evolutionary instruction generation (Evol-Instruct) using an LLM to progressively increase instruction complexity, followed by filtering and fine-tuning LLaMA on the evolved instructions.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not assess whether LLM-generated evolved instructions suffer from entropy decline or tail collapse compared to naturally diverse human-authored instructions.

title: From Quantity to Quality: Boosting LLM Performance with Self-Guided Data Selection for Instruction Tuning
venue: North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics
abstract: Ming Li, Yong Zhang, Zhitao Li, Jiuhai Chen, Lichang Chen, Ning Cheng, Jianzong Wang, Tianyi Zhou, Jing Xiao. Proceedings of the 2024 Conference of the North American Chapter of the Association for Computational Linguistics: Human Language Technologies (Volume 1: Long Papers). 2024.
matched_query: LIMA quality over quantity instruction tuning alignment
tldr: This work introduces a self-guided methodology for LLMs to autonomously discern and select cherry samples from open-source datasets, effectively minimizing manual curation and potential cost for instruction tuning an LLM.
manual_core_reason: From Quantity to Quality is a direct data-selection / instruction-tuning composition paper.
one_sentence_contribution: Proposes a self-guided data selection method for instruction tuning that prioritizes data quality over quantity to boost LLM performance.
gap_it_leaves: Does not address how self-guided selection scales to continual or domain-specific fine-tuning regimes.
manual_override_reason: From Quantity to Quality is a direct instruction-tuning data-selection / quality-vs-quantity anchor.
key_claim: Selecting just 10% of instruction tuning data using the Instruction-Following Difficulty metric yields models that outperform those trained on the full dataset.
methodology: Proposed IFD metric to score instruction samples by comparing model's expected responses with intrinsic generation capability; selected top cherry samples to fine-tune LLaMA2 models on Alpaca and WizardLM datasets.
limitation: The pre-experienced model training phase is inconvenient and makes the method hard to deploy directly in real-world scenarios.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether the quality degradation of web-scraped source data (e.g., entropy decline or diversity loss) limits the ceiling of any data selection strategy.

title: How Far Can In-Context Alignment Go? Exploring the State of In-Context Alignment
abstract: Recent studies have demonstrated that In-Context Learning (ICL), through the use of specific demonstrations, can align Large Language Models (LLMs) with human preferences known as In-Context Alignment (ICA), indicating that models can comprehend human instructions without requiring parameter adjustments. However, the exploration of the mechanism and applicability of ICA remains limited. In this paper, we begin by dividing the context text used in ICA into three categories: format, system prompt, and example. Through ablation experiments, we investigate the effectiveness of each part in enabling ICA to function effectively. We then examine how variants in these parts impact the model's alignment performance. Our findings indicate that the example part is crucial for enhancing the model's alignment capabilities, with changes in examples significantly affecting alignment performance. We also conduct a comprehensive evaluation of ICA's zero-shot capabilities in various alignment tasks. The results indicate that compared to parameter fine-tuning methods, ICA demonstrates superior performance in knowledge-based tasks and tool-use tasks. However, it still exhibits certain limitations in areas such as multi-turn dialogues and instruction following.
matched_query: LoRA QLoRA fine-tune data composition ablation alignment
one_sentence_contribution: Explores in-context alignment as a parameter-free alternative to fine-tuning for aligning LLMs with human preferences using structured demonstrations.
gap_it_leaves: Does not investigate how in-context alignment interacts with data composition or quality of demonstrations at scale.
key_claim: In-context alignment's EXAMPLE component is the most critical factor, though ICA significantly underperforms fine-tuning in multi-turn dialogue and instruction-following tasks.
methodology: Ablation experiments decomposing ICA prompts into FORMAT, SYSTEM, and EXAMPLE components; evaluated across Llama2-7B/13B/70B using GPT-4o as judge on justeval-instruct dataset.
limitation: The 70B model employed 4-bit GPTQ quantization due to resource constraints, which may affect results.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate how the quality or authenticity of demonstration examples (e.g., human-generated vs. web-scraped) affects in-context alignment performance.

title: Fine-tuning with HED-IT: The impact of human post-editing for dialogical language models
abstract: Automatic methods for generating and gathering linguistic data have proven effective for fine-tuning Language Models (LMs) in languages less resourced than English.Still, while there has been emphasis on data quantity, less attention has been given to its quality.In this work, we investigate the impact of human intervention on machine-generated data when fine-tuning dialogical models.In particular, we study (1) whether post-edited dialogues exhibit higher perceived quality compared to the originals that were automatically generated; (2) whether fine-tuning with post-edited dialogues results in noticeable differences in the generated outputs; and (3) whether post-edited dialogues influence the outcomes when considering the parameter size of the LMs.To this end we created HED-IT, a large-scale dataset where machine-generated dialogues are paired with the version post-edited by humans.Using both the edited and unedited portions of HED-IT, we fine-tuned three different sizes of an LM.Results from both human and automatic evaluation show that the different quality of training data is clearly perceived and it has an impact also on the models trained on such data.Additionally, our findings indicate that larger models are less sensitive to data quality, whereas this has a crucial impact on smaller models.These results enhance our comprehension of the impact of human intervention on training data in the development of high-quality LMs.
matched_query: "From Quantity to Quality" instruction tuning data selection
one_sentence_contribution: Investigates the impact of human post-editing of machine-generated dialogues on fine-tuned dialogical language model quality, finding quality improvements from human intervention.
gap_it_leaves: Does not examine cost-effectiveness of human post-editing at large scale or for non-dialogical tasks.
key_claim: Human post-editing of machine-generated dialogues improves both perceived data quality and downstream fine-tuned model performance for Italian dialogical models.
methodology: Created HED-IT corpus of machine-generated Italian dialogues paired with human post-edited counterparts; fine-tuned Pythia models in three sizes; evaluated via automatic metrics and human evaluation.
limitation: Resource is only in Italian; data was generated by ChatGPT which may produce hallucinations; testing was limited to Pythia models only.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether human post-editing addresses systematic diversity loss or distributional biases inherent in machine-generated training data.

title: Fine-tuning with HED-IT: The impact of human post-editing for dialogical language models
abstract: Automatic methods for generating and gathering linguistic data have proven effective for fine-tuning Language Models (LMs) in languages less resourced than English. Still, while there has been emphasis on data quantity, less attention has been given to its quality. In this work, we investigate the impact of human intervention on machine-generated data when fine-tuning dialogical models. In particular, we study (1) whether post-edited dialogues exhibit higher perceived quality compared to the originals that were automatically generated; (2) whether fine-tuning with post-edited dialogues results in noticeable differences in the generated outputs; and (3) whether post-edited dialogues influence the outcomes when considering the parameter size of the LMs. To this end we created HED-IT, a large-scale dataset where machine-generated dialogues are paired with the version post-edited by humans. Using both the edited and unedited portions of HED-IT, we fine-tuned three different sizes of an LM. Results from both human and automatic evaluation show that the different quality of training data is clearly perceived and it has an impact also on the models trained on such data. Additionally, our findings indicate that larger models are less sensitive to data quality, whereas this has a crucial impact on smaller models. These results enhance our comprehension of the impact of human intervention on training data in the development of high-quality LMs.

one_sentence_contribution: Investigates the impact of human post-editing of machine-generated dialogues on fine-tuned dialogical language model quality, finding quality improvements from human intervention.

title: Enhancing Chat Language Models by Scaling High-quality Instructional Conversations
abstract: Fine-tuning on instruction data has been widely validated as an effective practice for implementing chat language models like ChatGPT. Scaling the diversity and quality of such data, although straightforward, stands a great chance of leading to improved performance. This paper aims to improve the upper bound of open-source models further. We first provide a systematically designed, diverse, informative, large-scale dataset of instructional conversations, UltraChat, which does not involve human queries. Our objective is to capture the breadth of interactions that a human might have with an AI assistant and employs a comprehensive framework to generate multi-turn conversation iteratively. UltraChat contains 1.5 million high-quality multi-turn dialogues and covers a wide range of topics and instructions. Our statistical analysis of UltraChat reveals its superiority in various key metrics, including scale, average length, diversity, coherence, etc., solidifying its position as a leading open-source dataset. Building upon UltraChat, we fine-tune a LLaMA model to create a powerful conversational model, UltraLLaMA. Our evaluations indicate that UltraLLaMA consistently outperforms other open-source models, including Vicuna, the previously recognized state-of-the-art open-source model. The dataset and the model will be publicly released\footnote{\url{https://github.com/thunlp/UltraChat}}.
matched_query: LIMA quality over quantity instruction tuning alignment
one_sentence_contribution: Introduces UltraChat, a large-scale diverse high-quality instructional conversation dataset for fine-tuning chat LLMs without human queries.
gap_it_leaves: Does not systematically ablate quality vs. quantity trade-offs within the dataset or assess long-term model collapse from synthetic data.
key_claim: Scaling diverse, high-quality synthetic instructional conversation data (UltraChat) significantly improves open-source chat language models without requiring human queries.
methodology: Systematic generation of multi-turn instructional conversations using ChatGPT across three sectors (questions about the world, creative writing, assistance on existing materials from C4), then fine-tuning LLaMA to produce UltraLLaMA
limitation: Evaluation of LLM response quality may have biases; ChatGPT used for automatic evaluation instead of GPT-4; dataset only supports English currently.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not examine whether synthetically generated instructional data suffers from diversity loss or entropy decline compared to authentic human conversational data.

title: What Are the Internal and External Applications of Conversational Data in Large Language Models?
abstract: As Large Language Models (LLMs) have become increasingly integrated into the internet, the collection and use of conversational data from LLM interactions have introduced both new potential and concerns.The paper reviews both internal and external applications of LLM conversational data in light of current regulations.The paper also highlights the conversational dataset's preprocessing step of filtering and labeling, which are shown to enhance the efficiency in both internal and external applications.Internally, this paper describes the use of conversational data for fine-tuning and model improvement.This paper will cover the main types of fine-tuning, including supervised fine-tuning, reinforcement learning from human feedback, and multi-turn dialogue supervised fine-tuning.Externally, conversational data supports user analytics and behavior predictions across both large and small populations.But both applications are heavily dependent on various elements of conversational data around quality and quantity.In addition, constraints on accessing high-quality data continue to hinder the development of conversation-based user analytics.The paper addresses different constraining factors that create a disparity between large and small AI companies, including current LLMs' privacy policies and governance frameworks, such as the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), the California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA), and the EU Digital Markets Act (DMA).Ultimately, the areas of improvement that emerge in this paper lead to a set of recommendations for SMEs and policymakers to leverage between LLMs' beneficial capabilities and privacy concerns.
matched_query: Self-Instruct WizardLM OpenAssistant instruction tuning data selection
one_sentence_contribution: Reviews internal and external applications of conversational data collected from LLM interactions, highlighting preprocessing steps like filtering and labeling under current regulations.
gap_it_leaves: Does not empirically evaluate the effect of different filtering strategies on downstream model performance.
key_claim: LLM conversational data has distinct internal applications (fine-tuning, RLHF) and external applications (user analytics, behavior prediction), both dependent on data quality filtering and labeling.
methodology: Literature review of internal and external applications of LLM conversational data in light of current regulations
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not address whether recycling LLM-generated conversational data for training causes information degradation or diversity loss over time.

title: RefGPT: Dialogue Generation of GPT, by GPT, and for GPT
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have attained the impressive capability to resolve a wide range of NLP tasks by fine-tuning high-quality instruction data. However, collecting human-written data of high quality, especially multi-turn dialogues, is expensive and unattainable for most people. Though previous studies have used powerful LLMs to generate the dialogues automatically, they all suffer from generating untruthful dialogues because of the model hallucination. Therefore, we propose a method called RefGPT to generate enormous truthful and customized dialogues without worrying about factual errors caused by the model hallucination. RefGPT solves the model hallucination in dialogue generation by restricting the LLMs to leverage the given reference instead of reciting their own knowledge to generate dialogues. Additionally, RefGPT adds detailed controls on every utterance to enable high customization capability, which previous studies have ignored. On the basis of RefGPT, we also propose two high-quality dialogue datasets generated by GPT-4, namely **RefGPT-Fact** and **RefGPT-Code**. RefGPT-Fact is a dataset with 100k multi-turn dialogues based on factual knowledge and RefGPT-Code has 76k multi-turn dialogues covering a wide range of coding scenarios. Our code and datasets are released in https://github.com/mutonix/RefGPT.
matched_query: Self-Instruct WizardLM OpenAssistant instruction tuning data selection
one_sentence_contribution: Proposes RefGPT, a method for generating truthful multi-turn instruction dialogues grounded in reference documents to reduce hallucination in LLM fine-tuning data.
gap_it_leaves: Does not address how grounding in reference documents scales across diverse domains or languages.
key_claim: RefGPT generates truthful multi-turn dialogues by grounding LLM generation on reliable references, effectively mitigating hallucination in synthetic dialogue datasets.
methodology: Reference-grounded dialogue generation using GPT-3.5/GPT-4 with Wikipedia and GitHub repositories as references, with customizable dialogue structure, style, and content templates; truthfulness evaluated via human evaluation and GPT-4 with reference-augmented checking
limitation: Generated dialogues inherit factual errors, biases, typos from references (Wikipedia) and bugs/malicious code from GitHub; LLM biases are also reflected in generated dialogues.
what_it_does_NOT_address: Does not investigate whether reference-grounded synthetic dialogues can substitute for authentic human social behavioral data in capturing naturalistic conversational patterns.
